Just now

--

What 2,850,244 live stores tell us about the hosted-commerce web.About webatla. webatla is a domain-intelligence platform. We crawl and fingerprint domains across the web and record which technology each site runs — the same idea as BuiltWith or Wappalyzer, with broader coverage. Every figure here is a real count from the webatla index, last refreshed 7 July 2026. The full write-up, with all charts, lives on the blog linked above.
Most claims about Shopify arrive as a mood: it’s everywhere, every DTC brand uses it, it ate e-commerce. This one arrives as a count. There are 2,850,244 websites made with Shopify live in the webatla index — 0.9% of every domain we track. This piece is about what that count contains, what it beats, and the two places the data will mislead you if you read it too fast — starting with the platform that quietly beats it.
What Shopify is, briefly
Shopify is a hosted (SaaS) commerce platform founded in 2006 and headquartered in Ottawa, Canada. A merchant does not install it on a server — the store runs on Shopify’s own infrastructure, behind a custom domain or a myshopify.com address. Hold onto that detail, because it is the key to reading everything below: a hosted platform centralizes hosting, keeps every store on a current version, and geolocates to the company rather than the customer. It is the structural opposite of a self-hosted WooCommerce store, and the two models leave different footprints in the index.
The count, and the one platform that beats it
2,850,244 is not a survey estimate. It is the number of distinct live domains where webatla currently detects Shopify. And it comes with a twist that contradicts the usual narrative: Shopify is not the most-used commerce platform in the index. That title belongs to WooCommerce, the open-source plugin for WordPress, at 3,377,108 sites — about 527,000 ahead. The precise, defensible statement is this: Shopify is the largest hosted commerce platform on the web, and WooCommerce is the largest self-hosted one. Everything after those two falls off a cliff — Magento (75,035), PrestaShop (70,633) and BigCommerce (26,333) are each in the tens of thousands, against millions for the two leaders.
The commerce landscape webatla sees
Widen the lens from dedicated platforms to commerce capability — every shopping cart the index can fingerprint, including the store features inside general site builders — and the field reshuffles again.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Live sites by commerce technology. Source: webatla index, July 2026.By this broader measure, commerce on Squarespace tops the chart at roughly 7.6M sites, ahead of WooCommerce (~3.5M), Shopify (2.85M) and Wix eCommerce (~1.4M). But the ranking is a trap if you read it as “most stores.” A site builder with a commerce feature switched on is not the same as a purpose-built shop; many of those Squarespace and Wix sites are portfolios or brochures that merely could sell. Shopify’s 2.85M domains are, almost without exception, real stores. That is the difference between counting capability and counting commerce — and it is why per-domain, Shopify carries more transactional weight than a builder’s optional cart.
Who these stores actually are
A count is abstract until you see the names inside it. The index surfaces a representative spread of live sites made with Shopify — from Shopify’s own domain to niche marketplaces to nine-figure DTC brands.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Example Shopify stores. Source: webatla index, July 2026myfonts.com (a typeface marketplace), fleetfeet.com (a running-shoe chain) and fashionnova.com (a fashion brand) sit in the same index as shopify.com itself. The range is the point: hosted commerce is not just tiny side-hustles or just enterprise flagships — it is both, on one platform, which is exactly why the 2.85M number is large and dense at the same time.
Where the data will mislead you, part one: geography
Now the two honesty checks. Pull Shopify’s country breakdown and 93.8% of stores appear to be in Canada. They are not. Shopify is a hosted platform headquartered in Canada, and its stores run on Shopify’s own infrastructure, so the domains geolocate to that infrastructure rather than to the merchant. Canada here is Shopify’s address, not the shop owner’s. It is the mirror image of self-hosted WordPress sites, which resolve to thousands of independent hosts around the world. For any SaaS platform, the country column measures where the servers are, not where the customers are — so discard it when you want demand.
Where the data will mislead you, part two: the year-over-year drop
The second trap is the trend line. The index shows Shopify down -32.61% year over year, which reads like decline until you look at the neighbours: WooCommerce fell -61.70%, Magento -70.02%, PrestaShop -90.31% and BigCommerce -92.02%. Whole categories do not lose 60–90% of their real installs in one window for the same reason. This is a recount — a shift in how the index counts live installations between crawls — not stores closing en masse. Two things survive the scrutiny: Shopify is still the #1 hosted platform, and at -32.61% it lost the least of any major commerce platform in the recount, which is what you would expect from hosted stores that stay live and reachable rather than aging out like abandoned self-hosted installs.
The signal that does hold: domain endings
If geography and the trend line both need caveats, what can you trust? The TLD split, because merchants choose their own names. Among sites powered by Shopify, .com leads at 64.8%, but the distinctive part is what comes next: the commerce-only endings .store (5.0%) and .shop (4.0%), then .uk (2.7%) and .au (2.1%). Those .store and .shop domains barely register on general-purpose platforms; here they are second and third. That is the cleanest domain-level proof that this population is built to sell — and the .uk and .au endings that follow point to visible Shopify adoption in the UK and Australia.
What the number is actually good for
A count like this earns its keep when you act on it. If you are sizing the commerce market, Shopify and WooCommerce are the only two names that matter at scale — everything else is a rounding error. If you are choosing an integration target, that duopoly is your roadmap. And if you are reading platform data anywhere, Shopify is a clean case study in what to distrust: ignore the 93.8%-Canada geography, discount the raw year-over-year delta, and anchor on the counts and the TLDs. The real value of counting 2,850,244 websites made with Shopify is not the headline — it is that every claim built on top of it can be checked against the same crawl instead of taken on faith.
How the count is made
It is worth being explicit about method, because a headline number invites the question “counted how?” webatla does not poll a panel or model from a sample. We crawl domains at web scale and record where the Shopify fingerprint is actively served, then tally the distinct live domains that carry it. So 2,850,244 is a direct count of detections, not an estimate. The platform, TLD and geolocation figures all come from the same crawl, which is why they reconcile with each other — the .store/.shop share, the Canada geolocation and the WooCommerce comparison are three cuts of one dataset, not three separate studies stitched together.
Three things to take from the data
If you act on one section, make it this one:

• The real-store web is a duopoly. Shopify and WooCommerce cover almost every active store; Magento, PrestaShop and BigCommerce sit in the tens of thousands, not the millions. Build for the two that matter.

• Trust the TLD, not the country. For a hosted platform, geolocation measures infrastructure. The .store/.shop cluster and the UK/Australia tilt are the honest read on where Shopify demand sits.

• Anchor on counts, not deltas. The -32.61% year-over-year figure is a recount that hit every platform; the count and the share are the numbers that survive scrutiny. Shopify lost the least of any major platform in that recount.
How to get the list of websites made with Shopify
You can work with this data directly instead of quoting a chart. The Shopify technology page on webatla is a live, filterable list of every store we detect — filter by TLD, isolate .store and .shop, and compare Shopify against WooCommerce and Magento in one view. The complete write-up, with the platform and geolocation charts, is on the source article: webatla.com/blog/websites-made-with-shopify.
